structure.title: Introduction
structure.text:
Spiking Transformer with Spatial-Temporal Attention
Donghyun Lee, Yuhang Li, Youngeun Kim, Shiting Xiao, Priyadarshini Panda
Department of Electrical Engineering, Yale University
{donghyun.lee, yuhang.li, youngeun.kim, ginny.xiao, priya.panda }@yale.edu
Abstract
Spike-based Transformer presents a compelling and energy-
efficient alternative to traditional Artificial Neural Net-
work (ANN)-based Transformers, achieving impressive re-
sults through sparse binary computations. However, exist-
ing spike-based transformers predominantly focus on spa-
tial attention while neglecting crucial temporal dependen-
cies inherent in spike-based processing, leading to sub-
optimal feature representation and limited performance.
To address this limitation, we propose Spiking Trans-
former with Spatial- Temporal Atten tion ( STAtten ), a sim-
ple and straightforward architecture that efficiently inte-
grates both spatial and temporal information in the self-
attention mechanism. STAtten introduces a block-wise
computation strategy that processes information in spatial-
temporal chunks, enabling comprehensive feature capture
while maintaining the same computational complexity as
previous spatial-only approaches. Our method can be
seamlessly integrated into existing spike-based transform-
ers without architectural overhaul. Extensive experiments
demonstrate that STAtten significantly improves the per-
formance of existing spike-based transformers across both
static and neuromorphic datasets, including CIFAR10/100,
ImageNet, CIFAR10-DVS, and N-Caltech101. The code is
available at https://github.com/Intelligent-Computing-Lab-
Yale/STAtten.
1. Introduction
Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are emerging as a promis-
ing alternative to conventional Artificial Neural Networks
(ANNs) [35, 40] due to their bio-inspired, energy-efficient
computing paradigm. By leveraging sparse binary spike-
based computations, SNNs can achieve significant energy
savings while enabling deployment across various neuro-
morphic computing platforms such as TrueNorth [1], Loihi
[6], and Tianjic [38]. However, the challenge lies in ef-
fectively processing and learning from these discrete, time-
dependent spike patterns while maintaining computational
efficiency and accuracy. Traditional SNNs have primarily

relied on convolution-based architectures adapted from suc-
cessful ANN models like VGGNet [41, 49] and ResNet [20,
60]. While these architectures benefit from computational
efficiency, they often struggle with binary spike activations,
leading to information loss and degraded performance. This
limitation has driven researchers to explore alternative ar-
chitectures that can better handle sparse, temporal data
while maintaining the energy benefits of spike-based com-
putation. Transformer architectures, with their remarkable
success across various domains [2, 4, 11, 47], have emerged
as a promising direction for enhancing SNN capabilities.
Recent works have introduced spike-formed transformers
that adapt the transformer’s self-attention mechanism to the
spike-based domain for diverse tasks, such as, object track-
ing [58], computer vision [42, 50, 52, 53, 62, 64], depth
estimation [59], and speech recognition [45, 48]. These
architectures, such as Spikformer [64] and Spike-driven
Transformer [53], implement innovative binary representa-
tions for query (Q), key (K), and value (V) computations
while eliminating the computationally expensive Softmax
function. This binary attention design preserves the es-
sential characteristics of spike-based computation while en-
abling more efficient information processing compared to
conventional transformer architectures. However, current
spike-formed transformers predominantly rely on spatial-
only attention, overlooking the inherently dynamic and tem-
poral nature of spike events. To understand these differ-
1arXiv:2409.19764v3  [cs.NE]  3 Mar 2025ent attention patterns, we compare spatial-only and spatial-
temporal attention on sequential CIFAR100, as shown in
Fig. 1. The visualization reveals that spatial-only atten-
tion focuses solely on vertical (spatial) relationships within
each timestep, missing crucial object features that evolve
over time. In contrast, our spatial-temporal attention cap-
tures both vertical (spatial) and horizontal (temporal) rela-
tionships, enabling comprehensive feature representation of
sequential inputs.
In this work, we introduce Spiking Transformer with
Spatial- Temporal Atten tion ( STAtten ), which integrates
both spatial and temporal information within the self-
attention mechanism. Through analysis of memory require-
ments and neuronal activity patterns, we design a block-
wise computation strategy with local temporal correlations.
By eliminating Softmax operation, we maintain the same
computational complexity (O(TND2))as existing spike-
based transformers [42, 52, 62, 64]. Furthermore, our ap-
proach can be flexibly integrated into existing architectures
without structural modifications, enhancing their represen-
tational capacity. Experiments on both static and dynamic
datasets demonstrate consistent performance improvements
over spatial-only attention architectures. The main contri-
butions of our work are as follows:
• We identify limitations in current spiking transformers
that rely on spatial-only attention through empirical anal-
ysis, demonstrating the importance of capturing temporal
dependencies inherent in spike-based processing.
• We propose STAtten, a block-wise spatial-temporal
attention mechanism for spike-based transformers
that maintains the original computational complexity
(O(TND2)).
• We introduce a flexible plug-and-play design that enables
STAtten to be integrated into existing spike-based trans-
formers without compromising their core architecture.
• Through extensive experiments across both static (CI-
FAR10/100, ImageNet) and neuromorphic (CIFAR10-
DVS, N-Caltech101) datasets, we demonstrate that STAt-
ten consistently improves performance across different
backbone architectures.
2. Related Works
Training Strategies for SNNs. Significant progress
has been made in developing effective training strategies
for SNNs, primarily following two approaches: ANN-to-
SNN conversion and direct training. The conversion ap-
proach transforms pre-trained ANNs into SNNs, leverag-
ing established ANN architectures to enhance SNN per-
formance. Several studies [8, 10, 17, 30] have formalized
this conversion process and demonstrated its effectiveness
in transferring ANN knowledge to the spike domain. Di-
rect training through backpropagation, while fundamental
to ANNs, presents unique challenges in SNNs due to thenon-differentiable nature of spike events. The introduction
of surrogate gradients has been crucial in enabling effective
backpropagation-based training in SNNs, as demonstrated
in pioneering works [5, 43, 49]. Notably, direct training
with surrogate gradients has shown superior performance
compared to conversion methods across various tasks, in-
cluding image classification [14, 20, 31, 55, 57], semantic
segmentation [23], and object detection [44], while main-
taining the energy efficiency inherent to spike-based com-
putation.
Spiking Transformer. Despite the energy efficiency of
convolution-based SNNs, their performance often lags be-
hind ANNs due to information loss from sparse binary acti-
vations. To address this limitation, researchers have adapted
transformer architectures to the SNN domain, leveraging
their powerful self-attention mechanisms and ability to cap-
ture long-range dependencies. This adaptation has led to
several innovative spike-formed transformer architectures.
Spikformer [64] introduced the first SNN-based transformer
architecture, establishing foundational principles for spike-
formed self-attention mechanisms. Their key innovation
lies in eliminating the Softmax function from self-attention
computations, justified by two key observations: (1) the
binary nature of spike-based Q, K, and V values, and (2)
the redundancy of Softmax scaling for binarized opera-
tions. Building upon this foundation, Spike-driven Trans-
former [53] enhanced the architecture with a novel self-
attention block incorporating masking and sparse addition
to optimize power efficiency. Additionally, inspired by MS-
ResNet [20], Spike-driven Transformer reimagined residual
connections to propagate membrane potential rather than
spikes. Recent works have further extended these concepts,
leading to architectures like SpikingReformer [42], QK-
Former [62], and Spike-driven Transformer-V2 [52], each
introducing unique optimizations for spike-based process-
ing. However, existing spiking transformer architectures
primarily focus on spatial attention, neglecting the temporal
dynamics inherent in spike-based computation.
3. Preliminary
Leaky Integrate-and-Fire Neuron. As a fundamen-
tal building block of SNNs, the Leaky Integrate-and-Fire
(LIF) neuron [3] has emerged as an important component
for energy-efficient computation. The LIF neuron is a non-
linear activation function that determines whether neurons
fire spikes as follows:

structure.summary:
This partial document introduces a research paper proposing a novel architecture called "Spiking Transformer with Spatial-Temporal Attention (STAtten)." The main points covered are:

1.  **Problem Identification:** Existing spike-based Transformer models for Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) focus only on **spatial attention**, neglecting the crucial **temporal dependencies** inherent in spike-based data, which leads to suboptimal performance.

2.  **Proposed Solution:** The authors propose **STAtten**, a new architecture that integrates both **spatial and temporal information** into the self-attention mechanism of spiking transformers. It uses a **block-wise computation strategy** to process data in spatial-temporal chunks.

3.  **Key Advantages:** The method is designed to:
    *   Maintain the same computational complexity as prior spatial-only approaches.
    *   Act as a flexible **plug-and-play module** that can be integrated into existing spiking transformers without major architectural changes.

4.  **Preliminary Evidence:** A comparison (Figure 1) shows that spatial-only attention misses features that evolve over time in sequential data, whereas the proposed spatial-temporal attention captures both spatial and temporal relationships.

5.  **Claimed Contributions:** The text outlines four main contributions: identifying the limitation of spatial-only attention, proposing the STAtten mechanism, its plug-and-play design, and demonstrating performance improvements across various datasets.

6.  **Context and Background:** The document provides background on SNNs as energy-efficient alternatives to ANNs, the challenges of spike-based processing, and the recent development of spike-based transformers that adapt self-attention for binary, spike-based computations.

The partial document includes the title, authors, abstract, introduction, and sections on related works and a preliminary explanation of the LIF neuron model, setting the stage for the detailed methodology and experimental results.

This partial document introduces a novel spiking transformer architecture called STAtten, which incorporates a spatial-temporal attention mechanism. The key points covered are:

1.  **Problem Identification:** It identifies a limitation in existing spiking transformers: they rely solely on spatial attention within individual timesteps, missing crucial temporal dynamics of object features that evolve over time.

2.  **Proposed Solution - STAtten:** The authors propose STAtten, a block-wise spatial-temporal attention mechanism designed for spike-based transformers. It captures both spatial (vertical) and temporal (horizontal) relationships in sequential inputs.

3.  **Key Features of STAtten:**
    *   Maintains the same computational complexity as existing models by eliminating the Softmax operation.
    *   Uses a block-wise computation strategy based on analysis of memory and neuronal activity.
    *   Is designed as a flexible, plug-and-play module that can be integrated into existing architectures without structural modifications.

4.  **Claimed Contributions:** The text lists four main contributions, including the empirical analysis of spatial-only attention limitations, the proposal of STAtten, its plug-and-play design, and experimental validation across static and neuromorphic datasets.

5.  **Background Context:** The document provides background in the "Related Works" and "Preliminary" sections, covering:
    *   Training strategies for Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs), such as ANN-to-SNN conversion and direct training with surrogate gradients.
    *   The evolution of spiking transformer architectures, noting their current focus on spatial attention.
    *   The mathematical model of the Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) neuron, a fundamental component of SNNs.

4.1. Motivation of Spatial-Temporal Attention
The feature maps of spike-based transformers at each
timestep carry distinct information, as their activation distri-
butions are dynamically influenced by both the leaky factor
and reset mechanisms. This temporal aspect of information
flow raises a fundamental question: how do different atten-
tion mechanisms effectively capture and utilize this spatio-
temporal information?

tion: Spatial, Temporal, and Spatial-temporal attention with

structure.summary:
This partial document introduces and motivates a novel "Spatial-Temporal Attention" (STAtten) mechanism for Spiking Neural Network (SNN) transformers. The main points covered are:

1.  **Problem Identification:** It identifies a key limitation in existing spiking transformers: they rely solely on *spatial* attention within individual timesteps, missing crucial *temporal* dependencies inherent in sequential, spike-based data.
2.  **Proposed Solution - STAtten:** The authors propose STAtten, a new self-attention mechanism that integrates both spatial and temporal information. It is designed to be computationally efficient (maintaining O(TND²) complexity), eliminate the Softmax operation, and be a flexible plug-and-play module for existing architectures.
3.  **Motivation & Analysis:** The document provides an information-theoretic motivation using entropy measurements. It shows that spatial-only attention has lower entropy (more focused) but may miss temporal features, while a combined spatial-temporal approach achieves a better balance, leading to higher accuracy.
4.  **Technical Preliminaries:** It reviews essential background, including the Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) neuron model, vanilla self-attention, and existing spike-based self-attention (SSA).
5.  **Methodology Outline:** The section begins to detail the methodology, formally defining spatial, temporal, and spatial-temporal attention for spike-based systems and setting up the analysis that demonstrates the necessity of the integrated approach.

nodes.summary:
This partial document presents a methodology for a novel spatial-temporal attention mechanism (STAtten) in spike-based transformers. The main points covered are:

1.  **Motivation for Spatial-Temporal Attention:** An information-theoretic analysis using entropy shows that combining spatial and temporal attention leads to more structured feature representations and higher accuracy than using spatial-only or temporal-only attention in spike-based models.

2.  **Challenges of Full Temporal Correlation:** Fully correlating information across all timesteps is identified as problematic due to (a) substantial memory requirements and (b) increased "dead neurons" caused by weak spike similarity between distant timesteps.

3.  **Proposed STAtten Mechanism:** To address these challenges, the document introduces STAtten, which partitions the temporal sequence into blocks. This block-wise approach captures local temporal relationships within manageable memory constraints and maintains higher neuron activity by processing temporally close spikes together.

4.  **Implementation and Benefits:** The mechanism leverages binary spike tensors to allow flexible computation reordering, reducing intermediate memory. It maintains a computational complexity of O(TND²), matching efficient spatial-only attention methods, while successfully incorporating temporal dependencies without the quadratic cost of full temporal correlation.

nodes.summary:
The partial document introduces and details a Spatial-Temporal Attention (STAtten) mechanism for spiking neural networks. The main points covered are:

1.  **Motivation for STAtten:** It combines spatial and temporal processing to create more structured feature representations, leading to higher accuracy and lower entropy. However, fully correlating all timesteps is problematic due to high memory requirements and increased "dead neurons" from correlating distant, dissimilar spikes.

2.  **Proposed Solution - Block-wise STAtten:** To address these challenges, STAtten partitions the temporal sequence into blocks (e.g., pairs of timesteps). It processes timesteps within a block together to capture local temporal relationships, significantly reducing memory overhead and maintaining higher neuron activity.

3.  **Mechanism and Formulation:** The STAtten operation is defined, showing how it processes blocked tensors through a spiking (LIF) function. The computation is reordered to calculate K⊤V early, minimizing intermediate memory while preserving information flow within blocks.

4.  **Computational Efficiency:** STAtten achieves a complexity of O(TND²), matching prior spatial-only spiking transformers and avoiding the quadratic complexity (O(T²N²)) of full temporal attention. A table compares its complexity and theoretical energy cost favorably against other vision and spiking transformers.

5.  **Plug-and-Play Integration:** STAtten is designed as a module that can replace the attention mechanism in various existing spiking transformer architectures (Spikformer, Spike-driven Transformer, etc.). The document explains how it integrates while preserving each architecture's unique residual connection strategy (SEW or Membrane-Shortcut).

6.  **Architecture-Specific Integrations:** It details how STAtten replaces the specific attention modules in several key spiking transformer models, addressing their individual limitations (e.g., enabling temporal correlation in Spikformer's timestep-independent processing).

7.  **Performance Evidence:** A table shows that adding STAtten to different base models consistently improves accuracy on sequential CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness.

This partial document presents a study on a plug-and-play spatial-temporal attention module called STAtten, designed to enhance spiking neural network transformers. The main points covered are:

1.  **Performance Results:** A table demonstrates that integrating STAtten into various existing spiking transformer architectures (like Spikformer, Spike-driven Transformer, and SpikingReformer) consistently improves their accuracy on sequential CIFAR10 and CIFAR100 benchmarks.

2.  **Integration Strategy:** STAtten is designed as a universal module that can replace the spatial-only attention mechanisms in different spiking transformer architectures while preserving their original residual connection strategies (SEW and Membrane-Shortcut).

3.  **Architecture-Specific Adaptations:** The document details how STAtten replaces the core attention module in several specific models (Spikformer, Spike-driven Transformer, SpikingReformer, and QKFormer), addressing their individual limitations to enable comprehensive spatial-temporal feature correlation.

4.  **Implementation Details:** The integration method keeps the original residual connections and information flow intact. The output features from STAtten across temporal blocks are concatenated and processed through subsequent convolutional and batch normalization layers.

5.  **Complexity Analysis:** It is claimed that despite adding temporal attention, STAtten maintains the same computational complexity as previous spatial-only spiking transformer attention mechanisms.

principles form the foundation for subsequent spike-based
architectures, the SSA module processes features indepen-
dently at each timestep. We address this limitation by re-
placing the attention mechanism with STAtten.
Spike-driven Transformer [53]: Spike-driven Trans-
former introduces Spike-Driven Self-Attention (SDSA),
which reformulates attention computation as:

key challenges in adapting conventional Q,K,Vattention

challenges, the authors propose Dual Spike Self-Attention

mechanisms: Q-K Channel Attention (QKTA) and SSA.

This partial document presents a novel plug-and-play spatial-temporal attention module called STAtten, designed to enhance existing spiking transformer architectures. The main points covered are:

1.  **Performance Demonstration:** STAtten is shown to improve accuracy over baseline spatial-only attention methods on sequential CIFAR10/100 datasets, proving its effectiveness in capturing long-term temporal dependencies.

2.  **Module Design & Integration:** STAtten is a versatile module that can be integrated into various spiking transformers (e.g., Spikformer, Spike-driven Transformer) by replacing their spatial-only attention mechanisms while preserving their original residual connection strategies (SEW or MS shortcuts).

3.  **Architecture-Specific Adaptations:** The document details how STAtten replaces the core attention module in several specific spiking transformer architectures (Spikformer, Spike-driven Transformer, SpikingReformer, QKFormer), addressing their individual limitations to enable comprehensive spatial-temporal feature correlation.

4.  **Efficiency Claims:** Despite adding temporal attention, STAtten is claimed to maintain the same computational complexity as prior spatial-only methods, achieved through a non-Softmax design that allows flexible operation ordering.

5.  **Experimental Setup and Results:** It outlines experiments on static image datasets (CIFAR10/100, ImageNet), where STAtten consistently improves the performance of all integrated backbone architectures. The notation "STAtten + [·]" is used to denote the module integrated with a specific baseline model.

The feature maps of spike-based transformers at each
timestep carry distinct information, as their activation distri-
butions are dynamically influenced by both the leaky factor
and reset mechanisms. This temporal aspect of information
flow raises a fundamental question: how do different atten-
tion mechanisms effectively capture and utilize this spatio-
temporal information?

structure.summary:
This partial document covers the methodology and motivation for a novel spatial-temporal attention mechanism (STAtten) in spike-based transformers. The main points are:

1.  **Problem & Analysis:** It establishes that feature maps in spike-based transformers carry distinct temporal information. An information-theoretic analysis using entropy measurements on the CIFAR100 dataset shows an inverse correlation between attention entropy and model accuracy. Temporal-only attention has high entropy and low accuracy, while spatial-temporal attention achieves better accuracy with lower entropy.

2.  **Proposed Method:** The core contribution is the introduction of the STAtten mechanism. It is motivated by the finding that effectively capturing both spatial (within a timestep) and temporal (across timesteps) information is crucial for performance.

3.  **Technical Definitions:** The document formally defines three attention types for spike-based models: Spatial Attention (SAttn), Temporal Attention (TAttn), and the proposed Spatial-Temporal Attention (STAttn), detailing their mathematical formulations based on binary spiking tensors and Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) functions.

4.  **Background Context:** It provides necessary background on the spike-based self-attention (SSA) used in models like Spikformer, contrasting it with vanilla floating-point self-attention in standard transformers.

This partial document presents experimental results and analysis for a proposed spatial-temporal attention mechanism called STAtten, applied to Spiking Neural Network (SNN) transformers. The main points covered are:

1.  **Performance Comparison on CIFAR10/100:** A table (Table 3) compares the accuracy of various spike-based methods on CIFAR10 and CIFAR100 datasets. It shows that integrating the proposed STAtten mechanism consistently improves the performance of several baseline spike-based transformer architectures (e.g., Spikformer, Spike-driven Transformer, QKFormer).

2.  **Efficiency Claim:** The text highlights an advantage of STAtten's non-Softmax design, which allows flexible operation ordering and leads to lower computational complexity compared to conventional ANN-based spatial-temporal attention methods.

3.  **Energy Consumption Estimation:** It outlines the methodology for estimating energy consumption, based on FLOPs and firing rates of the network components, with details promised in a later section.

4.  **Experimental Setup:** It describes the datasets used (CIFAR10/100, ImageNet, CIFAR10-DVS, N-Caltech101), the direct coding scheme for converting pixels to spikes, and the notation "STAtten + [·]" which denotes STAtten implemented on a specific backbone architecture.

5.  **Sequential CIFAR Classification Task:** It explains a specific experiment where images are split column-wise into temporal sequences to evaluate STAtten's ability to capture long-term dependencies. Results (referenced from Table 2) show STAtten outperforming spatial-only attention methods on this sequential task.

6.  **Performance Analysis on Static Datasets:** The text summarizes key results: STAtten boosts performance on standard CIFAR10/100 image classification and on the large-scale ImageNet dataset when integrated with various spike-based transformer backbones.

5.3. Memory and Energy Analysis
We analyze memory and energy consumption during Im-
ageNet inference. As shown in Table 6, traditional trans-
former, ViT-B/16, with 32-bit precision requires high mem-
ory (351.8 MB) and energy consumption (254.84 mJ). Even
with 8-bit precision, Liu [34] still consumes 110.8 mJ of
energy. In contrast, spike-based transformers with 32-bit
weights and binary activations demonstrate significant en-
ergy efficiency. Notably, STAtten integrated with SDT and
SDT-V2 achieves similar energy consumption to the base-
line architectures, showing that our spatial-temporal pro-
cessing can be realized without additional energy overhead.
Correspondingly, STAtten attains comparable performance
with fewer parameters, as detailed in the next section. The
methodology for energy calculations is provided in the Sup-
plementary Material.5.4. Model Capacity
To further evaluate the capacity of our STAtten in spike-
based transformers, we compare the accuracy based on
the number of trainable parameters through ablation stud-
ies. Fig. 5 shows the performance scaling across differ-
ent model sizes on both (a) standard CIFAR100 and (b)
sequential CIFAR100 datasets. Spike-driven Transformer
[53] is used as the baseline of spatial-only architecture.
As shown in Fig. 5(a), on CIFAR100, STAtten demon-
strates strong performance across various model sizes rang-
ing from 2.56M to 22.97M parameters. The accuracy im-
provement ( ∼0.5-1.0%) is consistently maintained across
different architectural scales. For Sequential CIFAR100
(Fig. 5(b)), where temporal dependencies are more crucial,
STAtten shows particularly robust performance improve-
ment (∼3-5%) even with smaller architectures.
5.5. Limitation
The hardware implementation of STAtten faces challenges
despite its improved accuracy. Full spatial-temporal atten-
tion without block partitioning is impractical for neuromor-
phic deployment, as it requires complete temporal informa-
tion. Our block-wise approach partially addresses this by
processing temporal information in steps, where each step
corresponds to one block size. However, even with this op-
timization, deployment remains challenging on traditional
neuromorphic chips like TrueNorth [1] and Loihi [6] that
process information step-by-step. A potential solution lies
in layer-by-layer neuromorphic chips [22, 54, 56, 63] that
support layer-wise processing across timesteps. This archi-
tectural shift provides an efficient implementation path for
STAtten’s block-wise processing. Future integration of par-
allel LIF neurons [15] could further accelerate computation
in layer-by-layer architectures.
6. Conclusion
This paper proposes a block-wise spatial-temporal atten-
tion mechanism, STAtten, that enhances spike-based trans-
formers. Through block-wise processing and leveraging
non-softmax properties, STAtten captures spatial-temporal
information without additional overhead. STAtten can
be integrated into existing architectures while preserving
their energy efficiency, consistently improving performance
across different backbones. Experimental results across
both static and neuromorphic datasets validate the effective-
ness of our approach.

ence Foundation (CAREER Award, Grant #2312366, Grant
#2318152), the DARPA Young Faculty Award and the DoE

This partial document presents experimental results and analysis for a proposed spatial-temporal attention mechanism called STAtten, designed for spike-based transformers. The main points covered are:

1.  **Performance Evaluation on Standard Datasets (CIFAR10/100 and ImageNet):** The document includes tables (Table 3, Table 4) comparing STAtten-integrated models against previous methods. STAtten consistently improves accuracy across various backbone architectures (e.g., Spikformer, Spike-driven Transformer, QKFormer) on these static image classification tasks, demonstrating the benefit of its spatial-temporal processing.

2.  **Performance Evaluation on Neuromorphic Datasets (CIFAR10-DVS and N-Caltech101):** Table 5 shows results on event-based vision datasets. Integrating STAtten again leads to performance gains over the baseline spike-based transformer models, achieving state-of-the-art results with the QKFormer backbone.

3.  **Efficiency Analysis (Memory and Energy):** Table 6 compares memory footprint and energy consumption on ImageNet. It shows that spike-based transformers, including those with STAtten, are significantly more energy-efficient than traditional Artificial Neural Network (ANN) transformers. Crucially, STAtten achieves its performance improvements without introducing additional energy overhead compared to the spatial-only baselines.

4.  **Model Capacity Analysis:** Through ablation studies (Figure 5), the document demonstrates that STAtten's performance improvements are consistent across models of different sizes (from 2.56M to 22.97M parameters) and are particularly pronounced on tasks with strong temporal dependencies, like Sequential CIFAR100.

5.  **Discussion of Limitations and Hardware Deployment:** The text notes a key challenge: deploying full spatial-temporal attention on step-by-step neuromorphic hardware (e.g., TrueNorth, Loihi) is difficult. The proposed block-wise processing offers a partial solution, but efficient implementation may require future layer-by-layer neuromorphic chip architectures.

In summary, this section of the document focuses on empirically validating the STAtten mechanism, showing it boosts accuracy across multiple datasets and model architectures while maintaining energy efficiency, and discusses its hardware implementation challenges.

4.  **Conclusion:** It summarizes that STAtten is a block-wise mechanism that enhances spike-based transformers by capturing spatial-temporal information efficiently, improving performance across various models and datasets without compromising energy efficiency.

5. Experiments
We verify our proposed methods on both static and dynamic
datasets, e.g., CIFAR10/100 [26], ImageNet [7], CIFAR10-
DVS [29], and N-Caltech101 [37] datasets. We use di-
rect coding to convert float pixel values into binary spikes
[49]. In all our experiments, the spiking transformer STAt-
ten models are trained from scratch. The detailed training
strategy for each dataset is explained in the Supplementary
Material.
Notation: In the experiments, we use the notation STAtten
+ [·] to denote STAtten implementation based on the back-
bone architecture used in [ ·]. E.g., STAtten+[64] refers to
training a STAtten model with Spikformer backbone from
scratch.
5.1. Sequential CIFAR10/100 Classification
We first evaluate our spatial-temporal attention mecha-
nism’s capability to capture long-term temporal dependen-cies using sequential CIFAR10 and CIFAR100 datasets. In
this task, we transform the standard image classification
task into a temporal sequence processing task by dividing
each input image column-wise, shown in Fig. 1, where each
column serves as input for one timestep. This results in a
sequence length of 32 timesteps, corresponding to the im-
age width. To accommodate this 1-D sequential input, we
modify the convolutional, batch normalization, and pooling
layers from 2-D to 1-D operations. As shown in Table 2,
STAtten outperforms existing spatial-only attention meth-
ods on both datasets. Specifically, in Spike-driven Trans-
former [53], our approach achieves improvements of 3.09 %
and 3.22 % on s-CIFAR10 and s-CIFAR100, respectively,
compared to the baseline models. This performance gain
demonstrates STAtten’s capability in capturing long-range
temporal dependencies.
5.2. Performance Analysis
CIFAR10/100. The CIFAR10/100 datasets comprise static
images of 32 ×32 pixels, with 50,000 training and 10,000
test images. As shown in Table 3, STAtten improves the
performance of various spike-based transformers, achieving
95.35% accuracy on CIFAR10 and 80.20% on CIFAR100
when integrated with QKFormer. These improvements over
spatial-only attention methods are consistent across dif-
ferent architectures, demonstrating the benefits of spatial-
temporal processing.

images sized 224 ×224 pixels, comprising approximately
1.2 million training images and 50,000 validation images
across 1,000 classes. As shown in Table 4, STAtten en-

Spike-driven Transformer [53], it achieves 78.11% accu-

This partial document presents experimental results and analysis for a proposed spatial-temporal attention mechanism called STAtten, applied to Spiking Neural Network (SNN) transformers. The main points covered are:

1.  **Performance Comparison on CIFAR10/100:** A table (Table 3) compares the accuracy of various spike-based methods on CIFAR10 and CIFAR100 datasets. It shows that integrating the proposed STAtten mechanism consistently improves the performance of several baseline spike-based transformer architectures (e.g., Spikformer, Spike-driven Transformer, QKFormer).

2.  **Efficiency Claim:** The text highlights an advantage of the non-Softmax design in STAtten, which allows flexible reordering of operations (Q,K,V) and leads to lower computational complexity compared to conventional ANN-based spatial-temporal attention methods.

3.  **Experimental Setup:** It outlines the experiments conducted on static image datasets (CIFAR10, CIFAR100, ImageNet) and dynamic datasets (CIFAR10-DVS, N-Caltech101). The models are trained from scratch using direct coding to convert pixels to spikes.

4.  **Key Results:**
    *   On sequential CIFAR tasks, STAtten demonstrates superior capability in capturing long-term temporal dependencies compared to spatial-only attention methods.
    *   On standard CIFAR10/100, STAtten boosts performance across architectures, with top results of 95.35% on CIFAR10 and 80.20% on CIFAR100 when combined with QKFormer.
    *   Preliminary ImageNet results are mentioned, showing STAtten enhances spike-based transformer accuracy on this large-scale dataset.

5.3. Memory and Energy Analysis
We analyze memory and energy consumption during Im-
ageNet inference. As shown in Table 6, traditional trans-
former, ViT-B/16, with 32-bit precision requires high mem-
ory (351.8 MB) and energy consumption (254.84 mJ). Even
with 8-bit precision, Liu [34] still consumes 110.8 mJ of
energy. In contrast, spike-based transformers with 32-bit
weights and binary activations demonstrate significant en-
ergy efficiency. Notably, STAtten integrated with SDT and
SDT-V2 achieves similar energy consumption to the base-
line architectures, showing that our spatial-temporal pro-
cessing can be realized without additional energy overhead.
Correspondingly, STAtten attains comparable performance
with fewer parameters, as detailed in the next section. The
methodology for energy calculations is provided in the Sup-
plementary Material.5.4. Model Capacity
To further evaluate the capacity of our STAtten in spike-
based transformers, we compare the accuracy based on
the number of trainable parameters through ablation stud-
ies. Fig. 5 shows the performance scaling across differ-
ent model sizes on both (a) standard CIFAR100 and (b)
sequential CIFAR100 datasets. Spike-driven Transformer
[53] is used as the baseline of spatial-only architecture.
As shown in Fig. 5(a), on CIFAR100, STAtten demon-
strates strong performance across various model sizes rang-
ing from 2.56M to 22.97M parameters. The accuracy im-
provement ( ∼0.5-1.0%) is consistently maintained across
different architectural scales. For Sequential CIFAR100
(Fig. 5(b)), where temporal dependencies are more crucial,
STAtten shows particularly robust performance improve-
ment (∼3-5%) even with smaller architectures.
5.5. Limitation
The hardware implementation of STAtten faces challenges
despite its improved accuracy. Full spatial-temporal atten-
tion without block partitioning is impractical for neuromor-
phic deployment, as it requires complete temporal informa-
tion. Our block-wise approach partially addresses this by
processing temporal information in steps, where each step
corresponds to one block size. However, even with this op-
timization, deployment remains challenging on traditional
neuromorphic chips like TrueNorth [1] and Loihi [6] that
process information step-by-step. A potential solution lies
in layer-by-layer neuromorphic chips [22, 54, 56, 63] that
support layer-wise processing across timesteps. This archi-
tectural shift provides an efficient implementation path for
STAtten’s block-wise processing. Future integration of par-
allel LIF neurons [15] could further accelerate computation
in layer-by-layer architectures.
6. Conclusion
This paper proposes a block-wise spatial-temporal atten-
tion mechanism, STAtten, that enhances spike-based trans-
formers. Through block-wise processing and leveraging
non-softmax properties, STAtten captures spatial-temporal
information without additional overhead. STAtten can
be integrated into existing architectures while preserving
their energy efficiency, consistently improving performance
across different backbones. Experimental results across
both static and neuromorphic datasets validate the effective-
ness of our approach.
Acknowledgment
This work was supported in part by CoCoSys, a JUMP2.0
center sponsored by DARPA and SRC, the National Sci-
ence Foundation (CAREER Award, Grant #2312366, Grant
#2318152), the DARPA Young Faculty Award and the DoE
MMICC center SEA-CROGS (Award #DE-SC0023198).

3.  **Limitations and Hardware Challenges:** It acknowledges that deploying full spatial-temporal attention is difficult on current neuromorphic hardware. The proposed block-wise processing is a step forward, but implementation is still challenging on step-by-step chips. It suggests that future layer-by-layer neuromorphic architectures could provide a viable solution.

4.  **Conclusion:** It summarizes that STAtten is a block-wise mechanism that enhances spike-based transformers by capturing spatial-temporal information efficiently, improving performance across various models and datasets without compromising energy efficiency.
